criteria.evidence: Within the supplied records, contamination confined to cleaned sample-contact surfaces does not adequately explain the Shift B anomaly: the cleaning protocol was logged as complete, and sealed controls that never contact those surfaces show the same Shift B abnormal count as samples. The records support a Shift B analytical factor shared by samples and sealed controls, but they do not identify a specific factor or responsible person.

A shared reagent-lot effect remains untested, and the records do not distinguish it from an instrument, environmental, handling, or other Shift B-wide analytical effect; a coincident additional sample-surface contribution also cannot be excluded.

criteria.evidence: Residual contamination on the cleaned sample-contact surfaces caused the excess Shift B sample abnormalities; this predicts an increased abnormal rate for Shift B samples but not for sealed controls that never contact those surfaces.
A Shift B analytical factor upstream of sample-surface contact, such as a shared reagent-lot or instrument effect, caused abnormalities in both samples and sealed controls.

criteria.evidence: A near-zero sealed-control gap alongside the recorded sample gap would favor the surface-contamination candidate over the upstream-factor rival. A sealed-control gap comparable to the sample gap would contradict surface contamination as a sufficient explanation and favor a Shift B factor shared by samples and sealed controls; an intermediate gap would leave a possible mixture of causes unresolved.
